title: System Selection Criteria
summary: Design engineer must consider temperature, humidity, air motion, air/water velocity, water quality, outdoor air quality, indoor air purity, air changes, acoustics, climate, mold prevention, capacities, redundancy, spatial requirements, environmental health and safety, security, first cost, ROI, energy consumption, operator labor, maintenance costs, serviceability, reliability, flexibility, controllability, replaceability, life-cycle analysis, sustainability, seismic protection, filtration, and changing codes.
evidence_quote: Occupant comfort (as defined by ASHRAE Standard 55), process heating, space heating, cooling, and ventilation criteria must be considered when selecting the optimum system(s), as well as the following: Temperature, Humidity, Air motion, Air/water velocity, Water quality and/or reuse, Outdoor air quality or purity, Indoor air purity or quality, Air changes per hour, Acoustics and vibration, Local climate, Mold and mildew prevention, Capacities (existing, proposed, and future expansion), Redundancy, Spatial requirements (present and future), Environmental health and safety design, Security, First cost, Return-on-investment cost, Energy consumption costs, Operator labor costs, Maintenance costs, Serviceability, Reliability, Flexibility, Controllability, Replaceability, Life-cycle analysis, Sustainability of design, Seismic protection, Filtration and filtration effects, Changing codes and standards

title: System Constraints
summary: Equipment and system constraints include performance limitations, code updates, available capacity, available space and access, available utility source, energy budget, equipment efficiency, operator knowledge, existing building occupants, and building architecture.
evidence_quote: Equipment and system constraints must be assessed and documented as part of the system analysis and selection recommendation report. These constraints may include the following: Performance limitations (e.g., temperature, humidity, space pressure, etc.), Code updates and/or new codes, Available capacity including equipment size, as well as ductwork and/or pipe size, Available space and access in and/or out with new or old equipment, Available utility source, Energy budget (e.g., conceptual Btu/h·ft2 per year), code-driven or targeted, Equipment efficiency, Operator knowledge and capabilities, Existing building occupants, Building architecture

title: Constructability Constraints
summary: Constructability issues include existing conditions, rigging equipment, demolition impact, maintaining occupancy, phasing installation, temporary HVAC, equipment availability, construction schedule, and construction budget.
evidence_quote: Some issues and concerns associated with constructability are: Existing conditions (e.g., floor load, access into and through a building), Rigging equipment into and out of a building to a designated area, Demolition and impact on adjacent space and existing systems in operation, Maintaining existing building occupancy, use of the building, and system operation, Ability to phase HVAC system installation, Temporary HVAC, Equipment availability (e.g., delivery lead time), Construction schedule, Construction budget

title: Decentralized System Characteristics
summary: Decentralized systems distribute cooling throughout the building, generally with direct-expansion cooling, electric or gas-fired heating, and occasionally oil-fired warm-air heating units.
evidence_quote: The common element is that the required cooling is distributed throughout the building, generally with direct-expansion cooling, electric, or gas-fired heating of air systems, and on occasion, oil-fired warm-air heating units.

title: Centralized System Characteristics
summary: Centralized systems are characterized by central refrigeration equipment with chilled-water distribution and central boiler equipment with hot-water distribution, including low-, medium-, and high-pressure steam plants and cogeneration systems.
evidence_quote: These systems are characterized by central refrigeration equipment/systems with chilled-water distribution and central boiler equipment/system with hot-water distribution. Other central systems include low-, medium-, and high-pressure steam plants and cogeneration central equipment/systems, or condenser water systems used with water-source heat pumps.

title: System Selection Report Contents
summary: The system analysis and selection report should include introduction, building program primary HVAC goals, criteria for selection, important factors, system integration, other HVAC goals, security and environmental health and safety criteria, building envelope, project timeline, basis of design, HVAC system analysis and selection matrix, system narratives, budget costs, and final recommendation(s).
evidence_quote: As the last step, the design engineer should prepare a system analysis and selection report in a format that incorporates the following: Introduction, Building program primary HVAC goals, Criteria for selection, Important factors, including advantages and disadvantages, System integration with other building systems, Other HVAC goals, Security and environmental health and safety criteria, Building envelope, Project timeline (design, equipment delivery, commissioning, training, and construction), Basis of design, HVAC system analysis and selection matrix, System narratives, Budget costs (first cost, operating cost, and energy cost), Final recommendation(s)

title: System Selection Matrix Scoring
summary: A numerical rating system such as 0 to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applicable, can provide a quantitative result for system selection.
evidence_quote: A numerical rating system such as 0 to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applicable, can provide a quantitative result.

title: Decentralized System Capacity Requirements
summary: A decentralized system usually requires each piece of equipment to be sized for zone peak capacity, unless the systems are variable-volume, and does not benefit as much from equipment sizing diversity as centralized systems.
evidence_quote: A decentralized system usually requires each piece of equipment to be sized for zone peak capacity, unless the systems are variable-volume. Depending on equipment type and location, decentralized systems do not benefit as much from equipment sizing diversity as centralized systems do.

title: Centralized System Capacity Diversity
summary: A central system usually allows the design engineer to consider HVAC diversity factors that significantly reduce installed equipment capacity, offering first-cost and operating-cost benefits.
evidence_quote: A central system usually allows the design engineer to consider HVAC diversity factors that significantly reduce installed equipment capacity. As a result, this offers some attractive first-cost and operating-cost benefits.

title: Decentralized System First Cost Advantage
summary: A decentralized system probably has the best first-cost benefit, enhanced by phasing in the purchase of equipment as needed.
evidence_quote: A decentralized system probably has the best first-cost benefit. This feature can be enhanced by phasing in the purchase of decentralized equipment as needed (i.e., buying equipment as the building is being leased/occupied).

title: Centralized System Operating Cost Advantage
summary: A central system usually has the advantage of larger, more energy-efficient primary equipment compared to decentralized system equipment, and allows staging of equipment operation to match building loads while maximizing operational efficiency.
evidence_quote: A central system usually has the advantage of larger, more energy-efficient primary equipment compared to decentralized system equipment. In addition, the availability of multiple pieces of HVAC equipment allows staging of this equipment operation to match building loads while maximizing operational efficiency.

title: Decentralized System Maintenance Cost
summary: A decentralized system can save maintenance cost when equipment is conveniently located and equipment size and associated components are standardized, but outdoor maintenance may be difficult during bad weather.
evidence_quote: A decentralized system can save maintenance cost when equipment is conveniently located and equipment size and associated components (e.g., filters) are standardized. When equipment is located outdoors, maintenance may be difficult during bad weather.

title: Centralized System Maintenance Cost
summary: The equipment room for a central system provides the benefit of being able to maintain HVAC equipment away from occupants in an appropriate service work environment, and there are usually fewer pieces of equipment to service.
evidence_quote: The equipment room for a central system provides the benefit of being able to maintain HVAC equipment away from occupants in an appropriate service work environment. Access to occupant work space is not required, thus eliminating disruption to the space environment, product, or process. Because of the typically larger capacity of central equipment, there are usually fewer pieces of HVAC equipment to service.

title: Decentralized System Reliability
summary: A decentralized system usually has reliable equipment, although the estimated equipment service life may be less than that of centralized equipment.
evidence_quote: A decentralized system usually has reliable equipment, although the estimated equipment service life may be less than that of centralized equipment.

title: Centralized System Reliability
summary: Centralized system equipment generally has a longer service life.
evidence_quote: Centralized system equipment generally has a longer service life.
